channel.title: OOIR Trends: Clinical Medicine (Medical Informatics)
channel.description: Aktuelle Paper-Trends im Bereich Clinical Medicine (Medical Informatics) von OOIR (mit Titel und Metadaten von Crossref)

item.title: Adolescent obesity in the digital age: navigating risks and opportunities

item.title: Big Tech and the Rise of Consumer-Facing Health AI Assistants
item.description: Feld: Clinical Medicine Kategorie: Medical Informatics Score: 68 Autoren: Tejas S Athni Journal: Journal of Medical Internet Research Veröffentlicht: 2026-04-30 DOI: 10.2196/99230 ISSN: 1438-8871 Tag der Erhebung (OOIR): 2026-05-20

item.title: Development and validation of a digital biomarker for peripheral artery disease
item.description:
Feld: Clinical Medicine Kategorie: Medical Informatics Score: 48 Autoren: Mattheus Ramsis, Ava J. Fascetti, Mustafa H. Naguib, Shamim Nemati, Pam R. Taub, Christopher A. Longhurst, Elsie G. Ross, Edward J. Wang Journal: npj Digital Medicine Veröffentlicht: 2026-05-12 Abstract: Abstract
Peripheral artery disease (PAD) is a common manifestation of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) that is underdiagnosed in clinical practice. Photoplethysmography (PPG) serves as a widely available tool that captures peripheral vascular physiology, yet the quantitative links between PPG signal characteristics and the presence of PAD are underexplored. In analyzing 5,237 legs from N = 2362 unique patients, we find significant correlations with multiple PPG features and the ankle-brachial index (ABI), a commonly used non-invasive diagnostic test for PAD. Using these explainable features, we develop a machine learning model to detect PAD solely from PPG features (AUC = 0.83) and develop an enhanced model incorporating clinical information (AUC = 0.85). Additionally, our model is highly generalizable, performing similarly across demographics and comorbidities. These findings represent an initial step toward identifying an accessible, physiologically grounded digital biomarker associated with PAD, and lay the foundation for prospective studies to evaluate performance across clinical workflows and reference standards.
DOI: 10.1038/s41746-026-02655-w ISSN: 2398-6352 Tag der Erhebung (OOIR): 2026-05-20

item.title: AI-Quantified Breast Arterial Calcification Can Predict Heart Disease Risk From Mammograms
